name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَسْـَٔلُكَ ٱلنَّاسُ عَنِ ٱلسَّاعَةِ ۖ قُلْ إِنَّمَا عِلْمُهَا عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيبًا (٦٣)
```

```english
The people question you concerning the Hour. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah.’ What do you know, maybe the Hour is near. (63)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَعَنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيرًا (٦٤)
```

```english
Indeed Allah has cursed the faithless and prepared for them a blaze, (64)
```

```arabic
خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۖ لَّا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلَا نَصِيرًا (٦٥)
```

```english
in which they will remain forever, and will not find any guardian or helper. (65)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ يَقُولُونَ يَـٰلَيْتَنَآ أَطَعْنَا ٱللَّهَ وَأَطَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَا۠ (٦٦)
```

```english
The day when their faces are turned about in the Fire, they will say, ‘We wish we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Apostle!’ (66)
```

```arabic
وَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّآ أَطَعْنَا سَادَتَنَا وَكُبَرَآءَنَا فَأَضَلُّونَا ٱلسَّبِيلَا۠ (٦٧)
```

```english
They will say, ‘Our Lord! We obeyed our leaders and elders, and they led us astray from the way.’ (67)
```

```arabic
رَبَّنَآ ءَاتِهِمْ ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ وَٱلْعَنْهُمْ لَعْنًا كَبِيرًا (٦٨)
```

```english
Our Lord! Give them a double punishment and curse them with a mighty curse.’ (68)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ ءَاذَوْا۟ مُوسَىٰ فَبَرَّأَهُ ٱللَّهُ مِمَّا قَالُوا۟ ۚ وَكَانَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ وَجِيهًا (٦٩)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not be like those who offended Moses, whereat Allah cleared him of what they alleged, and he was distinguished in Allah’s sight. (69)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَقُولُوا۟ قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (٧٠)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words. (70)
```

```arabic
يُصْلِحْ لَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ فَقَدْ فَازَ فَوْزًا عَظِيمًا (٧١)
```

```english
He will rectify your conduct for you and forgive you your sins. Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle will certainly achieve a great success. (71)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا ٱلْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَن يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا وَحَمَلَهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ ظَلُومًا جَهُولًا (٧٢)
```

```english
Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to undertake it and were apprehensive of it; but man undertook it. Indeed he is most unjust and ignorant. (72)
```

```arabic
لِّيُعَذِّبَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُنَـٰفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُنَـٰفِقَـٰتِ وَٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ وَٱلْمُشْرِكَـٰتِ وَيَتُوبَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَٱلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ ۗ وَكَانَ ٱللَّهُ غَفُورًا رَّحِيمًۢا (٧٣)
```

```english
Allah will surely punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women, and Allah will turn clemently to the faithful, men and women, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (73)
```

## (Commentary)

These verses mention the matter of the Hour and some of the punishment that will befall the disbelievers (الكافرين) therein. They command the believers (المؤمنين) to speak with right speech (القول السديد) and promise them a beautiful promise for doing so. Then, the Surah concludes by mentioning the Trust (الأمانة).

**His saying, the Exalted**: يَسْئَلُكَ النَّاسُ عَنِ السَّاعَةِ قُلْ إِنَّما عِلْمُها عِنْدَ اللهِ وَما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيباً **"The people ask you about the Hour. Say, ‘Its knowledge is only with Allah.’ And what do you know? Maybe the Hour is near."** (33:63). The verse mentions the people’s questioning about the Hour. They only intended for him to estimate the time of its occurrence for them, and whether it was near or far, as hinted at by the expression "the Hour" (الساعة). Thus, He commanded him to answer them by restricting the knowledge of it to Allah, the Glorified; this has been the consistent approach whenever it is mentioned in the Quran.

His saying: وَما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ تَكُونُ قَرِيباً **"And what do you know? Maybe the Hour is near"** is an increase in ambiguity, so they may know that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is like anyone else regarding the lack of knowledge of it, and that it is not among the secrets He confided to him while concealing it from the people.

**His saying, the Exalted**: إِنَّ اللهَ لَعَنَ الْكافِرِينَ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيراً **"Indeed Allah has cursed the faithless and prepared for them a blaze,"** (33:64). Cursing (لعن) the disbelievers is their removal from mercy (الرحمة). Preparation (الإعداد) is the making ready. The blaze (السعير) is the fire that has been ignited and flared up. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً لا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً **"to abide in it forever. They will find neither any guardian nor helper."** (33:65). The difference between a guardian (الولي) and a helper (النصير) is that the guardian (الولي) takes charge of the entire matter himself while the one under guardianship is set aside, whereas the helper (النصير) assists the one being helped in part of the matter, which is its completion. Thus, the guardian (الولي) manages the whole affair, while the helper (النصير) undertakes a portion of it. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: يَوْمَ تُقَلَّبُ وجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ يَقُولُونَ يا لَيْتَنا أَطَعْنَا اللهَ وَأَطَعْنَا الرَّسُولَا **"The day their faces are turned about in the Fire, they will say, ‘O would that we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Apostle!’"** (33:66). The turning about (تقلب) of their faces in the Fire refers to their transformation from one state to another—turning pale, then black, and becoming grim—or their being moved from one side to another so that the touch of the punishment is more profound, just as is done with roasted meat.

Their saying: يا لَيْتَنا أَطَعْنَا اللهَ وَأَطَعْنَا الرَّسُولَا **"O would that we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Apostle!"** is a statement made by them by way of regret and wishing.

**His saying, the Exalted**: وَقالُوا رَبَّنا إِنَّا أَطَعْنا سادَتَنا وَكُبَراءَنا فَأَضَلُّونَا السَّبِيلَا **"They will say, ‘Our Lord! Indeed we obeyed our masters and our elders, and they led us astray from the way."** (33:67). Masters (السادة) is the plural of master (سيد), which—according to *al-Majma‘*—is the exalted owner who possesses the management of the great mass (السواد الأعظم), meaning the largest group. Elders (الكبراء) is the plural of great/elder (كبير), and perhaps what is meant is the elder in age; for the common folk follow and imitate one of two men: either the master of the people or the eldest among them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: رَبَّنا آتِهِمْ ضِعْفَيْنِ مِنَ الْعَذابِ وَالْعَنْهُمْ لَعْناً كَبِيراً **"Our Lord! Give them a double punishment and curse them with a mighty curse.’"** (33:68). Two folds (الضعفان) means two equal measures.

They only asked for a double punishment for them because they went astray themselves and led others astray; for this reason, they also asked for a mighty curse (اللعن الكبير) upon them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ آذَوْا مُوسى فَبَرَّأَهُ اللهُ مِمَّا قالُوا وَكانَ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَجِيهاً **"O you who have faith! Do not be like those who offended Moses, whereat Allah cleared him of what they alleged, and he was distinguished in the sight of Allah."** (33:69). This is a prohibition against being like some of the Children of Israel, treating their Prophet with the same kind of offense (الإيذاء) with which the Children of Israel treated theirs. What is meant is not absolute offense by word or deed—though that is prohibited—rather, His saying: فَبَرَّأَهُ اللهُ **"whereat Allah cleared him"** testifies that it was an offense of the nature of accusation and slander, which required clearance (التبرئة) and exoneration (التنزيه) to remove.

Perhaps the silence regarding what they offended Moses (عليه السلام) with supports what has been mentioned in the hadith: that they said, "Moses does not have what men have," so Allah cleared him of their saying, as will be discussed.

The most plausible explanation regarding their offense to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is that it refers to the story of Zayd and Zaynab. If that is the case, then part of the offense to him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is what is found in many narrations of the story, recounting it in a manner that does not befit his sacred presence.

His saying: وَكانَ عِنْدَ اللهِ وَجِيهاً **"and he was distinguished in the sight of Allah"** means he possessed status (جاه) and station (منزلة). This sentence, in addition to containing a general clearance, provides the reason for Allah's clearing of him. This verse and the one following it have a type of connection to the verses prohibiting the offending of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

**His saying, the Exalted**: يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً **"O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words."** (33:70). **The upright** (السديد) comes from correctness (السداد), which is hitting the mark and being rightly guided. Thus, upright speech (السديد من القول) is that which combines correspondence to reality with not being vain (لغوا) or having an unlawful benefit, such as slander (النميمة) and the like. Therefore, the believer must examine the truth of what he speaks and ensure it is not vain or something that ruins a reconciliation.

**His saying, the Exalted**: يُصْلِحْ لَكُمْ أَعْمالَكُمْ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ فازَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً **"He will rectify your conduct for you and forgive you your sins. Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly achieved a great triumph."** (33:71). He made the rectification of deeds and the forgiveness of sins contingent upon the persistence in upright speech. This is because when the soul persists in upright speech, it detaches from lying, vain talk, and speech that results in corruption. By the rooting of this quality within it, it naturally detaches from indecency (الفحشاء), evil (المنكر), and vanity in action. At that point, the person's deeds are rectified, and he naturally regrets what he wasted of his life in destructive sins (موبقات الذنوب) if he had been afflicted by any of them; and regret suffices as repentance (توبة).

Allah then preserves him for the remainder of his life from plunging into perilous matters. If he intends any minor sins, Allah forgives him, for Allah the Exalted has said: إِنْ تَجْتَنِبُوا كَبائِرَ ما تُنْهَوْنَ عَنْهُ نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ **"If you avoid the major sins that you are forbidden, We will absolve you of your misdeeds"** (4:31). Thus, persistence in upright speech leads a person to the rectification of deeds and the forgiveness of sins by Allah's permission.

His saying: وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ فَقَدْ فازَ فَوْزاً عَظِيماً **"Whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle has certainly achieved a great triumph"** is a beautiful promise for performing all righteous deeds and avoiding all prohibitions, by making the great triumph contingent upon the obedience of Allah and His Messenger.

With this, the Surah essentially concludes in its meaning, because the obedience of Allah and His Messenger is the comprehensive word (الكلمة الجامعة) between all the preceding rulings, including obligations and prohibitions. The two following verses are like a completion of the meaning of this verse.

**His saying, the Exalted**: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمانَةَ عَلَى السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَها وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْها وَحَمَلَهَا الْإِنْسانُ إِنَّهُ كانَ ظَلُوماً جَهُولاً **"Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to bear it, and were apprehensive of it; but man undertook it. Indeed he is most unfair and ignorant."** (33:72) — until His saying — غَفُوراً رَحِيماً **"All-forgiving, All-merciful."** (33:73). The Trust (الأمانة) — whatever it may be — is something deposited with another to be preserved and then returned to the one who deposited it. Thus, this Trust mentioned in the verse is something Allah entrusted to man to preserve its integrity and uprightness, and then return it to Him, the Glorified, as He deposited it.

It is understood from His saying: لِيُعَذِّبَ اللهُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْمُنافِقاتِ **"so that Allah may punish the hypocrites, men and women..."** etc., that it is a matter whose bearing results in hypocrisy (النفاق), polytheism (الشرك), and faith (الإيمان). Thus, its bearers are divided, according to the manner of their bearing it, into hypocrites, polytheists, and believers.

It is inevitably a matter linked to the true religion (الدين الحق), through the adoption or non-adoption of which hypocrisy, polytheism, and faith occur.

Is it the true belief (الاعتقاد الحق) and the testimony to His oneness, the Exalted? Or the totality of belief and action, in the sense of accepting the true religion in its details regardless of acting upon it? Or the engagement in acting upon it? Or the perfection (الكمال) attained by man through engagement in one of these matters?

It is not the first, namely monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — for the heavens, the earth, and everything else declare His oneness and celebrate His praise. He, the Exalted, has said: وَإِنْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ إِلَّا يُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِهِ **"There is not a thing but celebrates His praise"** (17:44), while the verse explicitly states their refusal of it.

Nor is it the second, namely the true religion in its details, for the verse explicitly states that man bears it, whoever he may be, whether a believer or otherwise. It is clear that most of those who do not believe do not bear it and have no knowledge of it. By this, it becomes clear that it is not the third, which is the engagement in acting upon the true religion in detail.

Nor is it the perfection attained by him through engagement in monotheism, for the heavens, the earth, and others are currently speaking of monotheism and are engaged in it.

Nor is it the perfection resulting from taking the true religion and knowing it, since neither hypocrisy, polytheism, nor faith result from the mere true belief and knowledge of religious obligations, nor does it entail felicity or wretchedness. Rather, the effect only results from the commitment to the true belief and the engagement in action.

Thus, what remains is that it is the perfection (الكمال) attained by him through engagement in belief and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح), and traversing the path of perfection by ascending from the depths of matter to the peak of sincerity (الإخلاص) — which is that Allah purifies him for Himself so that none shares him with Him, and He, the Glorified, takes charge of his affairs. This is the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية).

So, what is meant by the Trust (الأمانة) is the divine guardianship (الولاية الإلهية), and its presentation (عرضها) to these things is its consideration as measured against them. What is meant by bearing it (حملها) and refusing it is the existence of the capacity (استعداد) and suitability for engagement in it, or the lack thereof. This meaning is what is capable of being applied to the verse. The heavens, the earth, and the mountains, despite their greatness, intensity, and power, lack the capacity for its attainment within them; this is what is meant by their refusal to bear it and their apprehension of it.

However, man, the most unfair (الظلوم) and ignorant (الجهول), did not refuse nor was he apprehensive of its weight and the greatness of its danger. He bore it despite its weight and the greatness of its danger. This resulted in man being divided, in terms of preserving the Trust or not through betrayal (الخيانة), into hypocrite, polytheist, and believer—unlike the heavens, the earth, and the mountains, for none of them is anything but a submissive believer.

If you say: Why did the All-Wise, the All-Knowing, place upon this most unfair and ignorant creature a burden that the heavens, the earth, and the mountains, despite their greatness, intensity, and power, could not bear? He knows that man is too weak to endure its bearing, and that he was only moved to accept it by his unfairness and ignorance, and emboldened by his delusion and heedlessness of the consequences. Thus, His charging him with the Trust at his own request due to his unfairness and ignorance is like appointing a madman to a general guardianship that the sane refuse and are apprehensive of, while the madman requests it due to the corruption of his intellect and the lack of uprightness in his thought.

I say: Although unfairness (الظلم) and ignorance (الجهل) in man are, in one respect, the basis for blame and reproach, they are the very things that make him suitable for bearing the Trust and the divine guardianship. For unfairness and ignorance are only attributed to one whose nature it is to be characterized by justice (العدل) and knowledge (العلم). The mountains, for example, are not characterized by unfairness or ignorance; it is not said "an unfair mountain" or "an ignorant mountain" because it is not correct to characterize it with justice or knowledge. Likewise, the heavens and the earth are not charged with unfairness and ignorance because it is not correct to characterize them with justice and knowledge, unlike man.

The Trust mentioned in the verse—which is the divine guardianship and the perfection of the attribute of servitude (العبودية)—is only attained through knowledge of Allah and righteous deeds, which is justice. These two attributes, namely knowledge and justice, only characterize a subject capable of ignorance and unfairness. Thus, man being in himself and according to his nature most unfair (ظلوماً) and ignorant (جهولاً) is what makes him suitable for bearing the divine Trust. Understand this.

The meaning of the two verses (1) corresponds in a way to the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ ثُمَّ رَدَدْناهُ أَسْفَلَ سافِلِينَ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فَلَهُمْ أَجْرٌ غَيْرُ مَمْنُونٍ **"We certainly created man in the best of forms; then We relegated him to the lowest of the low, except those who have faith and do righteous deeds: there will be an everlasting reward for them."** (95:4-6).

So His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمانَةَ **"Indeed We presented the Trust"**—meaning the divine guardianship and the seeking of perfection through the realities of the true religion in knowledge and action—and its presentation is its consideration as measured against these things.

His saying: عَلَى السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبالِ **"to the heavens and the earth and the mountains"**—meaning these great creations whose creation is greater than the creation of man, as He said: لَخَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ أَكْبَرُ مِنْ خَلْقِ النَّاسِ **"The creation of the heavens and the earth is surely greater than the creation of mankind"** (40:57). His saying: فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَها وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْها **"but they refused to bear it, and were apprehensive of it"**—their refusal to bear it and their apprehension of it is their lack of the suitability for engagement and their recoiling from accepting it. In the expression "bearing" (الحمل), there is a hint that it is a weight so heavy that the heavens, the earth, and the mountains cannot endure it.

His saying: وَحَمَلَهَا الْإِنْسانُ **"but man undertook it"**—meaning he possessed the suitability and the readiness for engagement in it despite his weakness and small size. إِنَّهُ كانَ ظَلُوماً جَهُولاً **"Indeed he is most unfair and ignorant"**—meaning unfair to himself, ignorant of the dire consequences and permanent destruction that would follow this Trust if he were to betray it.

In a more precise sense, it is because man is, in himself, devoid of justice and knowledge, yet capable of being clothed with what is poured upon him of that, and of ascending from the depths of unfairness and ignorance to the peak of justice and knowledge.

"Most unfair" (الظلوم) and "most ignorant" (الجهول) are two descriptions derived from unfairness and ignorance. Their meaning refers to one whose nature it is to be unfair and ignorant, similar to our saying: "a headstrong horse" (فرس شموس), "a bolting beast" (دابة جموح), and "purifying water" (ماء طهور)—meaning it is their nature, as stated by al-Razi. Or their meaning is the intensification (المبالغة) of unfairness and ignorance, as mentioned by others. The meaning is sound in either case.

His saying: لِيُعَذِّبَ اللهُ الْمُنافِقِينَ وَالْمُنافِقاتِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ وَالْمُشْرِكاتِ **"so that Allah may punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women,"** (33:73). The "Lām" is for the consequence (للغاية), meaning the consequence of this bearing was that Allah would punish the hypocrites, men and women, and the polytheists, men and women. This is because the one who betrays the Trust mostly feigns righteousness and trustworthiness, which is hypocrisy (النفاق), and rarely displays the betrayal openly. Perhaps the consideration of this meaning is what necessitated the mention of the hypocrites, men and women, before the polytheists, men and women, in the verse.

His saying: وَيَتُوبَ اللهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ وَكانَ اللهُ غَفُوراً رَحِيماً **"and that Allah may turn to the faithful, men and women, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful."** (33:73). This is conjoined to "so that He may punish" (لِيُعَذِّبَ), meaning the consequence of that was that Allah would turn to the believers, men and women. The turning (التوبة) from Allah is His returning to His servant with mercy. Thus, He returns with mercy to the person if he believes in Him and does not betray, and He takes charge of his affair—for He is the Guardian (ولي) of the believers—guiding him to Himself by veiling his unfairness and ignorance and adorning him with beneficial knowledge and righteous deeds, because He is All-Forgiving, All-Merciful.

If you say: What prevents the Trust from being interpreted as the religious obligation (التكليف), which is the true religion, and the bearing being the readiness and suitability, and the refusal being the lack thereof, and the presentation being the consideration of comparison? In that case, everything previously mentioned in the explanation of its application to the verse would apply.

I say: Yes, but the religious obligation is only sought because it is a preliminary to the attainment of divine guardianship and the realization of the attribute of complete servitude. Thus, it is the guardianship that is truly presented and sought for itself.

The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: لِيُعَذِّبَ اللهُ **"so that Allah may punish"** from the first person to the third person and the use of the Name of Majesty (اسم الجلالة) is to indicate that the consequences of all matters belong to Allah, the Glorified, because He is Allah.

The placement of the manifest noun in place of the pronoun in His saying: وَيَتُوبَ اللهُ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتِ **"and that Allah may turn to the faithful, men and women"** is to signal the perfection of care for them and the concern for their affair.

Regarding the interpretation of the Trust mentioned in the verse, there are various opinions:

It was said: What is meant by it are the religious obligations (التكاليف), the obedience to which necessitates entry into Paradise and the disobedience to which necessitates entry into the Fire. The presentation to the heavens, the earth, and the mountains is the consideration of them in relation to their capacity, and their refusal and apprehension is their lack of capacity for them, while man's bearing of it is his capacity. The speech is by way of allegory (التمثيل).

It was said: What is meant by it is the intellect (العقل), which is the basis for religious obligation and the criterion for reward and punishment.

It was said: It is the saying "There is no god but Allah" (لا إله إلا الله).

It was said: It refers to the limbs. The eye is a trust from Allah that must be preserved and not used except in what Allah, the Exalted, is pleased with; likewise the hearing, the hand, the foot, the private parts, and the tongue.

It was said: What is meant by it are the trusts of the people and the fulfillment of covenants.

It was said: What is meant by it is the gnosis of Allah (معرفة الله) and what it entails. This is the closest of opinions to the truth, returning by some approximation to what we have presented.

Likewise, there are different opinions regarding the meaning of presenting the Trust to them:

Among them: That the presentation is in its literal sense, except that what is meant by the heavens, the earth, and the mountains are their inhabitants. Thus, it was presented to the inhabitants of the heaven among the angels, and it was explained to them that in its betrayal is a great sin, so they refused it and feared bearing it. It was presented to man, and he did not refuse.

Among them: That it is in its literal sense. This is because when Allah created these celestial bodies, He created understanding within them and said to them: "I have ordained an obligation and created a Paradise for whoever obeys Me therein and a Fire for whoever disobeys Me therein." They said: "We are subjected to what You created us for; we cannot bear an obligation, nor do we seek reward or punishment." When He created Adam, He presented that to him, and he undertook it, being unfair to himself and ignorant of the dire consequence.

Among them: That what is meant by presentation (العرض) is comparison and juxtaposition. The gist of the speech is: We compared this Trust to the heavens, the earth, and the mountains, and it was weightier and heavier than them.

Among them: That the speech is by way of supposition and estimation. The meaning is: If We were to suppose that the heavens, the earth, and the mountains possessed understanding, and We presented this Trust to them, they would have refused to bear it and been apprehensive of it, but man undertook it.

By referring to what we have previously presented, the points of weakness and frailty in each of these opinions become apparent; so do not be heedless.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Salim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a hadith, he said: "And Allah does not curse a believer. Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لَعَنَ الْكافِرِينَ وَأَعَدَّ لَهُمْ سَعِيراً ـ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً لا يَجِدُونَ وَلِيًّا وَلا نَصِيراً** — 'Indeed Allah has cursed the faithless and prepared for them a blaze, to abide in it forever. They will find neither any guardian nor helper.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The Children of Israel used to say: 'Moses does not have what men have.' Whenever Moses wanted to perform a ritual bath, he would go to a place where no one could see him. One day, he was bathing on the bank of a river and had placed his clothes on a rock. Allah commanded the rock to move away from him until the Children of Israel looked at him. They then knew that it was not as they had said. So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ آذَوْا مُوسى** — 'O you who have faith! Do not be like those who offended Moses...'" the verse.

In *al-Majma‘*: "They differed regarding what Moses was offended with, according to several opinions:

First: That Moses and Aaron ascended the mountain and Aaron died. The Children of Israel said: 'You killed him.' So Allah commanded the angels, and they carried him until they passed by the Children of Israel with him. The angels spoke of his death until they knew that he had died. Thus, Allah cleared him of that. This is narrated from ‘Ali and Ibn ‘Abbas.

Second: That Moses was modest and private, bathing alone. They said: 'He only hides from us due to a defect in his skin, either leprosy or a scrotal hernia.' Once he went to bathe and placed his garment on a stone. The stone moved away with his garment, and Moses pursued it until the Children of Israel saw him naked, as one of the finest of men in physical form. Thus, Allah cleared him of what they said. This was narrated by Abu Hurayrah as a marfu‘ tradition."

**I say**: The first narration was also recorded in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mas‘ud, and the second from Anas and Ibn ‘Abbas.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Mardawayh extracted from Sahl ibn Sa‘d al-Sa‘idi, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) never sat upon this pulpit without reciting this verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللهَ وَقُولُوا قَوْلاً سَدِيداً** — 'O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and speak upright words.'"

**I say**: Something similar was also narrated from ‘A’ishah, Abu Musa al-Ash‘ari, and ‘Urwah.

In *Nahj al-Balaghah*: "Then there is the fulfillment of the Trust (الأمانة); for he who is not among its people has failed. It was presented to the built heavens, the spread earth, and the tall, anchored mountains—than which nothing is longer, wider, higher, or greater. If anything could have refused due to length, width, strength, or might, they would have refused. But they were apprehensive of the punishment and understood what he who is weaker than them—namely man—was ignorant of. Indeed, he is most unfair and ignorant."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Ishaq ibn ‘Ammar from a man from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمانَةَ** — "Indeed We presented the Trust..." the verse. He said: "It is the guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him)."

**I say**: What is meant by the guardianship (ولاية) of the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) is that for which he was the first to open the door among this nation—which is for man to be such that Allah, the Glorified, takes charge of his affair through his striving for Him by sincerity in servitude to Him. It does not mean guardianship in the sense of love or in the sense of Imamate, even if the outward meaning of some narrations suggests that by way of application and instantiation (الجري والانطباق).
